items.q: Do Group 1 metals get less reactive down the group like the halogens?
items.correct: No — Group 1 reactivity increases down the group because the outer electron is further from the nucleus and lost more easily.
items.misconception: Potassium is less reactive than lithium because bigger atoms are more stable.
items.random_wrong: All Group 1 metals are equally reactive because each loses one electron.
items.why: Students mix up the opposite trends of Groups 1 and 7; metals lose electrons more easily as atoms get larger, halogens gain them less easily.

items.q: Does copper fizz with dilute hydrochloric acid?
items.correct: No — copper is below hydrogen in the reactivity series, so it cannot displace hydrogen from a dilute acid.
items.misconception: All metals react with acid because metals are reactive substances.
items.random_wrong: Copper reacts with dilute acid to give off oxygen gas.
items.why: Students overgeneralise from zinc and magnesium demonstrations; position relative to hydrogen decides whether a metal reacts with acid.

items.q: When chlorine displaces bromine from bromide solution, is the chlorine oxidised?
items.correct: No — each chlorine atom gains an electron to form chloride, so chlorine is reduced; the bromide ions are oxidised.
items.misconception: Chlorine is oxidised because it is the reactive one causing the reaction.
items.random_wrong: Nothing is oxidised or reduced in a displacement reaction.
items.why: Students assume the 'active' reagent must be oxidised; tracking electron transfer shows the oxidising agent is itself reduced.

items.q: Which of these Group 1 metals is the most reactive?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Potassium. “Sodium” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: When chlorine water is added to potassium bromide solution, the mixture:
items.correct: Turns orange as bromine is displaced
items.why: Correct answer: Turns orange as bromine is displaced. “Shows no change” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which metal will displace copper from copper(II) sulfate solution?
items.why: Correct answer: Zinc. “Silver” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Salt + water + carbon dioxide. “Salt + hydrogen” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂. What volume of hydrogen at r.t.p. is produced from 0.50 mol of magnesium with excess acid? (1 mol of gas occupies 24 dm³)
items.why: Exact answer: 12 dm³. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
